input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-28T00:58:51.187Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference - Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated this as a success.

Let me look at their project files to understand the setup.

data.content:
Exit code 8 means "no tests discovered." Microsoft.Testing.Platform treats zero tests as an error by default, unlike VSTest.

The fix is a single property in your `.csproj`:

arguments.new_str:
<TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Return exit code 0 (instead of 8) when no tests are discovered -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

data.reasoningText:
The issue is clear. With Microsoft.Testing.Platform (enabled via `EnableMSTestRunner`), when no tests are discovered (because `INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` is not defined), the platform returns exit code 8, which signals "no tests found." VSTest used to return 0 in this case.

The fix is to set the MSBuild property `<TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>` in the project file. This tells Microsoft.Testing.Platform to return exit code 0 when no tests are discovered instead of 8.

Let me also verify this by checking if there's a way to run the project to confirm.

arguments.new_str:
<TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Return exit code 0 (instead of 8) when no tests are discovered -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

toolArgs.new_str:
<TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Return exit code 0 (instead of 8) when no tests are discovered -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

data.reasoningText:
Hmm, it still shows "Exit code: 8". Let me check - maybe the property name is different or maybe I need to check the MSTest documentation more carefully.

Wait, the output says "Exit code: 8" but the bash command itself exited with 0. That seems contradictory. Let me re-read...

Actually wait, looking more carefully, the "Exit code: 8" is printed by the `echo` command, which means `$?` captured 8 from `dotnet run`. But the bash session itself says `<exited with exit code 0>` which is the exit code of the whole bash command. Since `echo` always succeeds, the final exit code is 0.


So the test runner is still returning exit code 8, which suggests the property might not be recognized or needs a different name. I'm wondering if the correct approach is to use a command-line flag instead, or if the property name is different for the MSTest runner when using `EnableMSTestRunner`. Let me think through what the actual setting should be.

Looking at the Microsoft.Testing.Platform documentation, it seems like there are a few options: `--ignore-exit-code 8` for `dotnet test` in .NET 9+, or `--minimum-expected-tests 0` to change how the runner behaves. The property `TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests` might not exist yet, so I need to figure out which approach will actually work for the user's CI environment.

data.content: `--minimum-expected-tests` rejects 0, but `--ignore-exit-code 8` is the right option. Let me set it as an MSBuild property so it applies automatically in CI.

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Return exit code 0 (instead of 8) when no tests are discovered -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Treat "no tests discovered" (exit code 8) as success for conditional-compilation scenarios -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText: `--minimum-expected-tests` doesn't accept 0. But `--ignore-exit-code 8` should work. Let me try that and also figure out the MSBuild property to set it.

arguments.new_str:
<!-- Treat "no tests discovered" (exit code 8) as success for conditional-compilation scenarios -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.content:
**The fix:** Add `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` to your `.csproj`.

**What's happening:** Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns exit code **8** when zero tests are discovered (unlike VSTest which returned 0). The `--ignore-exit-code 8` flag tells the runner to return 0 instead. This property is picked up by `dotnet test`, which is what CI pipelines typically use.

If you run the test executable directly (not via `dotnet test`), you'd need to pass `-- --ignore-exit-code 8` on the command line instead.
